Com "todos os cisnes são brancos", ou temos um enunciado universal facilmente refutável ou um enunciado de causalidade infalsificável, porque ao aparecer um cisne negro, não sabemos se houve outros fatores que causaram a sua cor.
* Alan Watts: Causality and the Fallacy of Correlation :Philosophy:
About causality: "laws of nature are regularities observed". But you can see something regular because you use um meio regular: usas sempre a mesma régua. Se tu adquirisses outra perspetiva terias outra lei. Se tu não adquirires nenhuma perspetiva então chegas à verdade. Esta falácia da correlação acontece, por exemplo, entre dinheiro e wealth. Making a law is walking backwards, but you don't REALLY get to the beginning, therefore you just see correlation and you make an approximation.
* Sapiens: Language, Gossip, and the Collective Imagination :Philosophy:
Pensa-se que é a linguagem que levou à nossa ascensão sobre os outros animais. Um aspeto é que baseamo-nos em palavras, em vez de frases feitas, o que permite muitas combinações. Ou seja, pensamos na forma de variáveis, o que nos deixa fazer infinitamente mais com quase o mesmo espaço de memória, ou eficiência.
A fofoca é mesmo uma teoria sobre o sucesso da nossa língua, porque permite cooperação: sabemos e conhecemos melhor uns aos outros quando fofocamos. Talvez, na minha opinião, por termos em conta não só a relação que temos com uma pessoa, mas a relação que essa pessoa tem com as outras. 
Para a cooperação em larga escala, serve a ficção, acreditarmos em mitos comuns. Acreditamos em algo que se sobrepõe a todas as pessoas, de maneira que não precisamos de conhecê-las todas para trabalhar com elas. Somos uma equipa inconsciente, porque temos uma imaginação coletiva. Por isso somos ilimitados.

* Sapiens: The Experiencing Self, the Narrating Self, and Fiction :Philosophy:
Harari diz que nós não somos um "indivíduo", mas sim um "divíduo", e que temos dois operadores: o ser da vivência e o ser narrador. Relaciona isto com aquela experiência da dor do Kahneman, em que o narrador distorce a história para que ela se possa repetir. Por vezes o da vivência toma controlo e desfaz o que o narrador fez.
"Yet none of these things exist outside the stories that people invent and tell one another. There are no gods in the universe, no nations, no money, no human rights, no laws, and no justice outside the common imagination of human beings."
Now I remember that I had this "discussion" as a child, as many may have had. I distinctly remember asking my father if a company is its CEO. Else what is it? If said company pays taxes, someone has to pay them, right? So is the company the person who makes the payment?
"Telling effective stories is not easy" - hence it being hard to be a known author, and contaminating the meme pool, leaving a noticeable footprint.

* Sapiens: Specialization vs Generalization (Neanderthals vs Sapiens) :Life:
A capacidade de cooperação pode ser vista desta maneira: os neandertais, por exemplo, como estão todos separados têm de aprender tudo sozinhos, o que faz com que sejam decentes em tudo. Os Sapiens, em vez disso, especializam-se em certas coisas e, devido à crença coletiva, cooperam (como no capitalismo) com muito maior produtividade. De certa maneira, os neandertais estão a competir entre si, por isso, como é que iam ganhar aos Sapiens? Não dava, né.

* Sapiens: The Necessity of True Believers and Cognitive Dissonance :Philosophy:
And now it strikes me that some people think that climate change doesn't exist, just like some think that COVID doesn't exist. This is the collective belief in storytelling. They don't exist, we invented them. But the point is for everybody to believe in climate change, or COVID, so that we can (we think) stop bad things from happening. And these people can make the conspiracy that "oh, the government wants us to believe this narrative," which is true, but it's not the government or this or that, it's the nature of man, at least according to Harari.
Just like God, human rights and so on are all fictions of the imagination, even though useful, or actually just for being useful have they become things. "There is no god, but don't tell that to my servant, lest he will kill me at night" - Voltaire.
And it's freeing in some sense, it's some sort of truth to know this, and according to Harari there have to be some true believers, maybe because the structure of the fiction, like a building that was built, would be too fragile otherwise.
Harari brings forth the idea that cognitive dissonance is a feature, not a bug, in that for cultural evolution and for us to do what we do, essentially we hold some contradictory ideas. No one has to figure out all the necessary truths and make sense of the puzzle of the Universe; no one has to cover every hole in our reasoning to do something. We can have so many complexly related beliefs and act according to them. It makes us more prone to action, and as Harari says, dynamism and creativity.

* Sapiens: The Arrow of History and the Principle of Unity :Philosophy:
He points to an arrow of history, in which civilization tends to unity, despite bumps along the way, like the collapse of world-encompassing empires. Evolution makes a lot of new species, but eventually, it may become more likely that one starts to take over the market, the space, and dominates the landscape. Unity in this sense would be tending, in terms of living beings, towards the optimal fit for survival. 
Also, tending towards unity could just be the same as stating the infinite betting theorem. Which is to say, that eventually what is more likely is that something will have enough "power" to take over and bring the other stuff to zero, from where a stable equilibrium occurs. Like betting against an infinite machine, eventually, a long enough string of losses takes you to zero for definite. This unity principle can be seen through those games where you have a map and several circles like Agar.io or AI wars, that start small, and then eventually you are going to end up with one that dominated the rest. Or if you think of colors, a mix of all the colors is a higher entropy state, so the 2nd law of thermodynamics can also relate to this unity thing.

* Sapiens: Second-Order Chaotic Systems and the Power of Capitalism :Philosophy:
History is a second-order chaotic system, in that predictions will affect the outcome of history. It would require a view from another dimension, from outside, to even have a chance at steering the future. In retrospect, every system is in reality an infinite-order chaotic system, in that causal effects are propagated back and forth at the speed of light; they reverberate throughout the Universe, the world affects itself which affects itself. So just approximately are systems first-order chaotic.
History teaches us that we will have to make choices, that there isn't anything in the world (and so exists) that will tell us what we will do. We are inside the world.
Empty maps were a breakthrough in discovering the world, admittance of ignorance, which goes side by side with the Copernican principle, made the scientific revolution and imperialism possible.
It stuns me how much of a simple conceptual breakthrough it was: credit, loans, trust in the future, capitalism, that growth would fulfill itself. It's impressive how limited we are because we impose it. How constrained to move humans were, how much lies beyond the horizon, if just we have the bravery to make certain assumptions, or rather throw off our assumptions. We thought as if we were limited by our ignorance and not by the world.
We went from a zero-sum game, as in prey-predator dynamics and harmonic oscillators, to an exponential growth, flipped the sign of the differential equation. I want to live like this, in a capitalistic way, as in exponentially getting tighter and better, before the bubble bursts. Not just in terms of money, but knowledge and so on, productivity especially.

* Homo Deus: The Paradox of Knowledge and Shifting Directions :Philosophy:
O paradoxo do conhecimento, como Harari expressa, em que quanto mais sabemos mais imprevisíveis e indomáveis as coisas ficam. A meu ver, sabemos mais, deveríamos prever o que acontece melhor, mas nós próprios somos parte do sistema, e quanto mais sabemos, mais sabemos que não sabemos, e mais potencial então temos para fazer muita coisa e com ela muita bosta. 
Refere o marxismo, em que as previsões de Marx falharam precisamente porque ele quis mostrar que estavam certas. Ao escrever o livro que comprova que o mundo ia naquela direção, mudou a direção. Há este poder: ao escrever a história do capitalismo está a fazer com que as pessoas comecem a pensar em alternativas. Num certo sentido, ao escrevermos, por exemplo, um livro de 1000 páginas que se chame "Religião", estamos meio que a dizer que tudo sobre religião está ali, ou seja, esta é passada, e agora procuram-se alternativas. Eu explico isto, ou interpreto, como a contingência que Harari não chega a referir.

* Homo Deus: Intersubjective Meaning and the Pact of Modernity :Philosophy:
Harari fala do viés para acreditarmos que o nosso Deus e coisas assim são objetivos, enquanto que os da antiguidade não são, porque nos dão um sentido de vida. Como Searle provavelmente diria, podemos ter um sentido objetivo vindo de uma crença subjetiva.
"Os Sapiens dominam o mundo porque conseguiram criar uma teia intersubjetiva de sentido". Isto permite a cooperação em larga escala.
I would say an intersubjective map of meaning isn't sufficient, it has to be a specific one. If the map of meaning created had properties of pure individualistic competition, then everyone might have competed for the one spot in heaven. As we see it now, in Egypt, believing in the BS of the gods, in the sense that it was a creation, was actually very helpful. We only say this because we don't see that our creations of now are of the same nature.
"As empresas, o dinheiro e as nações só existem na nossa imaginação. Inventamo-las para que nos servissem. Então porque é que, quando nos damos conta, estamos a sacrificar as nossas vidas ao seu serviço?"
O pacto da modernidade é preferir o poder ao sentido. Estamos a aumentar o nosso poder cada vez mais, perdendo sentido no caminho, e, diz Harari, tentamos com o poder forçar a entrada de sentido.

* Homo Deus: Automation and Algorithms Knowing Us Better :Technology:
Sobre a automatização, Harari chega mesmo a dizer que, se nós somos algoritmos, poderão haver algoritmos no futuro que nos conheçam melhor que nós próprios, o que acabaria com o individualismo e com uma das premissas do liberalismo. Os algoritmos poderão tomar decisões por nós. A new kind of determinism, and the only one, as a matter of fact, that you feel.

* 21 Lessons: AI Replacing Intuition and the Control of Data :AI:
The next revolutions in tech, if they have to do with our thoughts, will be truly some of the most revolutionary things. I think our understanding of our inside is very shallow; we don't know much at all about our thoughts.
Harari talks about AI dominating the job market, and recognizing patterns as one thing it will replace humans at. He thinks intuition is pattern recognizing, so it won't be that hard to substitute. My issue is: will a skill of the future be to find the best minimizing function, defining goals essentially, for the AI?
And the AI knowing what you are thinking is beyond what humans have dealt with in the past. First, it's the biggest invasion of privacy, it destroys the last wall where you are by yourself. Then it has the potential to control your thoughts, perhaps even more alarming.
Algorithms will take decisions for us, will know us deeply and not in the human way, it will understand bluffs... Eventually, in the limit, one could say a master algorithm could legitimately trace out the future of mankind, as in predict the future and make it happen. Free will wouldn't just not exist for determinists, in the usual sense, but it also wouldn't exist in the coercion sense.
Talks about big corporations and data, and how the future might unfold. The big question he says we have to answer is "how do you regulate the ownership of data?" Are you serving power or truth?

* 21 Lessons: Adapting to Change, Rituals, and Not Fooling Yourself :Life:
Change will be a key component of the future. Be like water my friend; identity and individuality are like rocks, it can crash, or it can flow. Shapeless, formless, be one with yourself. Just like the process of truth in Bruce Lee: you seek it, you are aware of it, you have perception of it, you understand it, you experience it, you master it, you forget it, you forget how you got there, you repose into nothing, and thus you find truth at its roots.
Knowing yourself will be an important skill. That's where emotional intelligence comes in handy: the ability to know and control your emotions, cause those are among the most manipulable, and drive your actions sometimes.
The power of rituals: truth may be a liability, but for social manipulation, rituals are great. Make them fantasize, make them make the story for themselves, let them cultivate their imagination. And since pain is the realest of things, making people sacrifice for the hallucination is the most powerful.
You may not fool yourself, and you are the easiest person to fool. Now that I think about it, a low profile allows you to silently know way more than you show; you can see things that people don't know you can see. Build a big base inside yourself, cause building it outside you run the risk of not knowing you are already heading towards blindness.
Says he meditates 2 hours a day. I mean, be careful not to fool yourself with your own story, that's a lot of fucking time, don't sacrifice it like that lmao. Just like one shouldn't fool himself about the books he is reading. Don't lose yourself reading because "oh I want to be able to say I read the entire book." No, get what you want and get outta there. Skip pages, stop reading the book entirely. Be very selective. By not setting rigid filters, you get a range of books of equal value that would be unbearable and waste your time. Discriminate rigorously. Respect yourself. Not fooling yourself is respecting your self.

* The Master Algorithm: The Search for the Universal Learner :AI:
O autor fala de um Algoritmo Mestre que é a única coisa que tem de ser inventada. Acho que para isto acontecer, precisa de ser, em média, um pouco melhor que o humano e ter um ritmo de progresso um pouco melhor do que a média humana. Com isto assegurado, em média podíamos não inventar nada que o algoritmo faria o que faríamos, até um pouco melhor. 
"A aprendizagem automática é o que obtemos quando se junta a eficácia irrazoável da matemática com a eficácia irrazoável dos dados."
Faz a analogia dizendo que a ciência é composta por 3 fases, a fase Brahe, Kepler e Newton. A aprendizagem automática já é a melhor na Brahe, está a ficar a melhor no Kepler, e os Newtons, segundo o autor, terão de se meter a pau.
Diz que o grande poder dos algoritmos dos computadores é também o seu calcanhar de Aquiles, já que o maior problema da aprendizagem automática é o overfitting, em que se encontram padrões até onde não há.

* The Master Algorithm: Induction as the Inverse of Deduction :Math:
Afirma que a indução é o inverso da dedução, e que na história, quando procuramos inversos de operações conhecidas, descobrimos coisas novas e relevantes. Para tornar certas contas de aritmética possíveis (tipo 4-7), sacaram-se os números inteiros; com a divisão, números racionais; com as raízes de negativos, números complexos.
O outro exemplo que dá é: Sócrates humano, humano mortal, logo Sócrates mortal. E na indução temos o inverso. Sócrates humano, Sócrates mortal, o que falta? Falta fazer uma generalização, neste caso pode ser que todos os humanos são mortais. Newton formulou leis da indução, e uma delas é "tudo o que for verdade para tudo o que já vimos é verdade para tudo no Universo".

* The Master Algorithm: Connectionists and the Sigmoid Curve :AI:
Para os conexionistas, o importante é fazer com que o computador faça o que o cérebro faz. Embora computadores possam ter tantos transístores como neurónios no cérebro, neste último há um trabalho em paralelo. Para eles, o que é preciso é ter conexões como os neurónios têm. É na minha opinião um processamento de informação descentralizado. The key perhaps is not in the neuron, but the global interaction between them.
He says that the sigmoid curve, com o tipping point (ponto de inflexão), is the most important curve in the world, describing so many processes of change, of evolution and revolution, transições de fase, and in them is the stimulation of neurons. This curve in my point of view is the curve of contingencies and consciousness. We gradually get aware of something, then we notice it brutally, we get conscious of our consciousness, and then we get progressively used to it, it fades and goes into our background. The author also points out that Newton's second law isn't actually true, it isn't linear; in relativity, it shows in reality the right half of the sigmoid curve.

* The Master Algorithm: Evolutionaries, Analogizers, and Overfitting :AI:
Os evolucionistas criam algoritmos que, por evolução, ao chegarem a um determinado ponto, sofrem recombinação. Através da fitness function, vão sobrevivendo apenas alguns algoritmos. O problema é claramente os mínimos locais novamente. Pode dar-se o caso de que o leque de funções que tínhamos não estar de todo perto da função certa.
Os analogistas, às vezes, embora sejam preguiçosos (não buscam uma teoria unificada, apenas veem analogia e fazem assim), são mais eficientes. Uma regressão linearzinha por vezes é pior do que simplesmente procurar na base de dados um caso o mais verossimilhante possível e fazer o mesmo. Um dos grandes problemas disto é com atributos irrelevantes que só adicionam complexidade desnecessária (A maldição da dimensionalidade).
I now understand that the overfitting/underfitting dilemma is pretty much the same as the causation issue: if you don't see the whole system you only get causation approximately, and if you only select particulars, you get the impression of loss of injectivity.

* The Master Algorithm: Meta-Algorithms and Playing the Game :AI:
Uma das soluções de unificação proposta pelo autor é ter um meta-algoritmo que seja capaz de pesar as diferentes opiniões dos tipos de aprendizagem automática. Na minha opinião, precisamos (para não cometer uma aproximação) de uma regressão infinita de meta-algoritmos. This makes the poor assumption that all of these methods form a complete picture, as if there wasn't another method that will be discovered.
"Ensinar ao computador coisas a nosso respeito. Quanto mais lhe ensinarmos, melhor ele poderá servir-nos, ou manipular-nos. A vida é um jogo entre nós e os algoritmos que nos rodeiam. Podemos recusar-nos a jogar, mas então teremos de viver no século XXI como se vivêssemos no século XX. Ou podemos jogar para ganhar."
* The Origin of Species: Lessons from Darwin's Patience :Life:
Lessons from Darwin:
Recognize the underlying pattern, conceive bigger patterns than those you can see directly.
Travel around the globe. Find new philosophers of life, get exposed to new actions, ideas, ways of seeing things. Then, adapt your own.
Be patient. For 20 freaking years Darwin had the most revolutionary theory in evolutionary biology ever. Just like Kafka, somewhere on a desk were lying ideas that would change the world. But he waited, made it better, and when he published, he attacked with all the strength. Sheer patience and long-term thinking.
* The Meaning of It All: The Value of Truth and Interconnectedness :Philosophy:
"A verdade, a reles paga do cientista, descoberta pelos seus penosos esforços."
"Para qualquer coisa que olhemos, se olharmos suficientemente perto, estamos envolvidos com o Universo inteiro."
Há mais do que se pode conhecer do que o que se pode provar. Há uma parte muito maior da verdade que podemos conhecer do que a que podemos provar.

* The Meaning of It All: The Disease of Intellect and Beauty in Science :Philosophy:
Os cientistas da Idade Média. Eles próprios também fizeram um pouco de ciência, sem dúvida, tal como escreveram comentários sobre os grandes homens que viveram antes deles. E escreveram comentários sobre os comentários. Descreveram o que cada um escreveu sobre os outros. E continuam a escrever sobre estes comentários. "Escrever comentários é uma espécie de doença do intelecto." É importante ter novas ideias.
Feynman diz que os poetas reclamam dos cientistas por estes dizerem que as estrelas são meras bolas gasosas. Mas, na verdade, os cientistas é que apreciam a beleza, porque mesmo como meras bolas gasosas eles acham bonito, enquanto que os poetas têm de fazer metáforas e personificações para encontrar a beleza.

* The Meaning of It All: The Atomic Hypothesis and Fundamental Truths :Science:
Feynman diz que a frase que conveys the most information in the least words about the world is the atomic hypothesis. That if it were passed onto the next generation of creatures, it would pass the most info.
Maybe it's because it's the most true thing we know, but probably not. More because it is the most fundamental thing that we are pretty confident we know. But would it be the best thing we could pass to the next generation? Would they get the most out of this and not other pieces of information? In a sense, it's hard to imagine that another thing could be the most important. Essentially, what everything is at its core is the most revealing truth, the biggest pocket of reducibility.

* The Uninhabitable Earth: Climate Change, Adaptation, and Natural Extinction :Philosophy:
Diria que às vezes não nos podemos esquecer que nem toda a mudança é má, e convém relembrar que não sabemos como será o mundo com um aumento das temperaturas, não sabemos a sensibilidade do clima, não temos noção das nuances e complexidades que o clima tem. Às vezes uma coisa pode parecer má mas na verdade é neutra. O que aqui se faz é definir a sobrevivência dos humanos como "bom" e o que contrarie isso "mau". Se provocarmos a extinção de uma espécie, por exemplo abelhas, pomos em desequilíbrio o ecossistema global. Mas devemos ter em mente que o ecossistema global está mais ou menos sempre em desequilíbrio, porque o equilíbrio ideal é instável; qualquer pequeno desvio tira o planeta desse ponto, provocando movimento no sentido de lá voltar.
A alteração da migração dos bacalhaus, por exemplo, não parece ser assim tão significativa e indicativa de que estamos a estragar o planeta, apenas indica que algo está a mudar. O planeta é um sistema que se adapta, e os biomas têm-se alterado ao longo do tempo, e ninguém pensou que era mau ou que havia culpa em haver extinções em massa. Se houver uma extinção em massa por parte dos humanos, não nos esqueçamos que continua a ser uma extinção natural, e talvez aliens mais inteligentes que nós, vendo do exterior, achassem natural, e não mau propriamente, se isso acontecesse. O natural é que mais cedo ou mais tarde nos extingamos, ainda que não queiramos isso.

* The Uninhabitable Earth: Storytelling, Beliefs, and Narratives of the End :Life:
And now it strikes me that some people think that climate change doesn't exist, just like some think that COVID doesn't exist. This is the collective belief in storytelling. They don't exist, we invented them. But the point is for everybody to believe in climate change, or COVID, so that we can (we think) stop bad things from happening. And these people can make the conspiracy that "oh the government wants us to believe this narrative," which is true, but it's not the government, it's the nature of man, at least according to Harari.
Aponta que os humanos sempre escreveram histórias sobre o fim do mundo, e realmente é verdade. E isso ajuda-nos a preveni-lo porque temos uma crença coletiva de que algo mau pode acontecer e portanto indireta ou diretamente evitamo-lo ou tentamos evitá-lo. Tivemos de contar histórias sobre a nossa origem para nos confortarmos em sabermos o que somos bem lá no fundo, e histórias sobre o fim para sabermos o que podemos não ser, ou para termos algo palpável a que olhemos e possamos dizer: se não deixarmos isto acontecer, continuamos. É uma boa maneira de dizer que se nos extinguirmos, é culpa nossa, e não de uma força motriz misteriosa.

* The Uninhabitable Earth: Acknowledging the Problem and Climate Fiction :Life:
The author is also quick to point out that this is the case despite being right about your predictions about the end of the world being generally considered bad. So it is by the process of making predictions that would put us into the situation of being right about the bad, that we avoid this situation, because now we avoid it.
"Manda cá para fora e podes derrotá-lo." Ou: Admitting the existence of the problem is a good step towards solving it. Acknowledging the problem is a great way to start fixing it. Os dois servem para sabermos o que devemos fazer no agora.
Acha que quanto ao clima e ecologia há filmes e coisas assim, mas por alguma razão não vemos isto como um aviso do fim do mundo (Interstellar...). Por alguma razão muitos veem as histórias do clima como uma ficção, o efeito secundário do storytelling. Thinks we are engaged in a distraction which convinces us that everything will be fine. Diz que à medida que os efeitos climáticos se tornarem mais sérios, passamos de ficção para não-ficção. As he says, no one would want to consume fiction about a theme that is already real, just by looking at the window.

* The Uninhabitable Earth: Capitalism, Villains, and Unequal Futures :Life:
Também põe em evidência que o clima não é tão flashy. Todos fazem a narrativa do "onde estavas quando o Michael Jackson morreu" e não "onde estavas quando o carbono subiu das 400 partes por milhão". Mais tarde cita um autor que diz "É mais fácil imaginar o fim do mundo do que o fim do capitalismo". Com a automatização (IA), se perdermos o emprego lixamo-nos, porque embora a IA faça a produtividade maior, vai tudo para quem a fez. Isto pode ser porque já estamos na fase em que pensamos e escrevemos NO capitalismo, em vez de SOBRE o capitalismo.
Diz que também é difícil criar histórias com heróis e com vilões, e que apesar de já estarmos a ter consequências, ainda preferimos olhar para outras espécies como o urso polar, numa, diz o autor, "atitude estudada de impotência".
"O futuro já aqui está, o que não está é distribuído equitativamente" - William Gibson. Ou seja, muita da história do futuro já foi escrita, falta é concretizar-se (por exemplo, GPT-3, LaMDA, etc).

* The Uninhabitable Earth: The Arrow of History and the Illusion of Constant Progress :Philosophy:
Há a possibilidade, defende o autor, de isto da ecologia levar ao conflito, porque logicamente, se é uma coisa em que é preciso uma cooperação mundial, não dá para os países quererem ficar à frente uns dos outros. A China tem um rácio culpa/afetação muito maior que a Índia. Até historicamente, na revolução agrícola começou a haver mais conflitos.
O autor também fala da história, de que olhamos para a história sempre como progresso. E isto tem de ser assim, não dá para saber que no passado sabíamos menos, porque teríamos de nos lembrar do passado e do que sabíamos no passado. O autor diz que com isto do clima pode acontecer que vejamos um grande regresso, o que discordo. No passado também houve pandemias gigantes, e não pensamos que aí voltamos atrás. "Whatever doesn't kill you makes you stronger".
O autor já tinha dito que as pessoas "back in the day" achavam que não conseguiam controlar as coisas. Mas acho que isto é uma ilusão de quem vive no presente e vê o passado. Ao saber que existimos, quer dizer que correu bem automaticamente, e por isso vemos as coisas do passado como acidentes que nos fortaleceram. O futuro no presente é indeterminado. Se pensarmos que somos imunes ao não-progresso, então o risco é ainda maior.

* The Uninhabitable Earth: Desumanists and the Anthropic Principle :Philosophy:
O autor procede a falar de um grupo de pessoas, que se podem chamar desumanistas, que acham que o facto de não nos lembrarmos de que as histórias que criamos são histórias nos vai lixar; acham que temos de acordar para a realidade. Mesmo assim poderia ser argumentado que se acreditássemos nisso precisaríamos de cooperação, e a história em que acreditaríamos é a de que as histórias em que estávamos a acreditar eram meros mitos.
O autor chega a referir o princípio antrópico, e diz que "a ciência só pode ganhar perdendo". Discordo, porque é errado dizer que a ciência prevê a nossa extinção. Népia. A ciência inclui as outras possibilidades.

* O Alienista: A Ciência Acima de Tudo e a Casa Verde :Literature:
O livro de que vos falo foi escrito em 82 do século XIX, por Machado de Assis. A única nota inicial de autor é uma onde discute a utilização do vocábulo "reprochar", se é ou não um francesismo, e se soa bem. Enfim, coisas dele.
A história é sobre Simão Bacamarte, um psiquiatra. Acredita que a ciência (ou pseudociência) é o seu emprego único, e Itaguaí o seu Universo. Faz tudo pela ciência e com a ciência. Por exemplo, casou com uma mulher que nem é bonita nem é simpática, mas que reúne as condições fisiológicas para ter bons filhos.
Simão observa que naquele sítio ninguém cura os loucos. Devido a esta má exploração da psiquiatria, ambiciona ele criar um sítio, a Casa Verde, para onde vão todos os loucos, onde possa estudá-los e curá-los, a fim de desvendar a natureza da loucura e descobrir o remédio universal. E começam a chegar os considerados doidinhos. Um tal de João de Deus que diz ser o Deus João, que está convencido que se falar o mundo acaba; outro que se põe a fazer discursos académicos depois de todos os almoços.

* O Alienista: A Expansão da Loucura e a Revolta dos Barbeiros :Literature:
Mas o alienista quer mais, e chega a uma nova teoria, em que a sanidade é o perfeito equilíbrio das faculdades mentais, e tudo o resto é loucura. O estoque de amostras na Casa Verde multiplica-se, a tal ponto que 80 por cento da população está lá. Até o padre confessa que não sabia que havia assim tantos loucos no mundo. 
É tudo suave e bom até que a pessoa mais popular de Itaguaí lá vai parar. Um que tinha a mania de cumprimentar toda a gente. Qualquer idiossincrasia é digna de estudo, por isso cuidadinho com o que dizem porque o alienista vai-te pegar. Nem a mulher está safa, porque a ciência é imparcial. 
Começa-se a fazer sentir o ressoar de insatisfação no povo. Uma pessoa conspirou que seria inveja do Simão, e isso mereceu-lhe um lugar na galeria dos alucinados. As pessoas acabam por revoltar-se, e lideradas por um barbeiro, vão ao encontro do alienista. O barbeiro almeja chegar ao poder, e é mesmo aclamado de governador. No entanto, chegado ao poder, aproveita o alienista como um eliminador dos seus adversários políticos, o que leva o outro barbeiro da cidade a revoltar-se e tornar-se governador. Assim sendo, aprendemos que é preciso ter atenção aos barbeiros.

* O Alienista: O Perfeito Desequilíbrio e o Cativeiro de Si Próprio :Literature:
Mas, vá lá, Simão entende que estava errado, que a loucura estava afinal na perfeição, e que ser normal era atingir o perfeito desequilíbrio. O que é certo é que as pessoas são libertadas do cativeiro da Casa Verde, e até o barbeiro ganancioso decidiu que preferia a glória da tesoura à governação de Itaguaí. Epah, está tudo louco, só pode.
E pronto, viveu tudo feliz para s... oh não, esperem aí. Se pensam que Simão está acabado então ainda não conhecem o nosso alienista. Nota de seguida que no meio daquilo tudo é ele quem tem o equilíbrio perfeito das faculdades. Ele é afinal o primeiro exemplo da sua nova teoria (a tese levou à antítese que deu na síntese). Fecha-se na Casa Verde e vai-se observar através da tela áspera da ciência, quiçá curar-se. Acaba por morrer 1 ano mais tarde.

* O Alienista: Dogmatismo, Autoridade e o Viés Científico :Philosophy:
Um tema que sobressai na leitura é o poder e a autoridade. A verdade é que Simão sabe tanto, mas tem tão pouca sabedoria; está tão convicto, e ao mesmo tempo inconsciente do que está a fazer, o que o torna perigoso, porque com grande poder vem grande responsabilidade. As pessoas resistem a pô-lo em causa porque o veem como uma autoridade. Quando ele vai defender a criação da Casa Verde, fá-lo com tanta eloquência e certeza que as pessoas pensam que ele tem de saber o que está a fazer. Ele sabe que tudo sabe, esse é o primeiro grande erro. 
Machado critica ferozmente o dogmatismo e o cientifismo. Simão respeita mais os textos de medicina, a sua Torá, do que as suas vivências. O alienista nunca assume que está errado; pelo contrário, está certo e os outros estão a ser avaliados. Bacamarte está para todos os efeitos cego à realidade exterior, porque tem a grade científica sempre na frente. De pensar tanto, encontra imperfeições onde elas não estão. Nos comportamentos vê sintomas, nas manias vícios, na imaginação alucinação. Quase que assume a loucura de todos, incubindo o efeito placebo nos pacientes, enquanto enriquece e fica cada vez mais confiante no seu sistema. Faz da ciência o fim e da vida o meio, ao invés do contrário.

* O Alienista: O Formato da Obra e a Ironia de Machado :Literature:
Quanto ao livro em si, penso que é assumidamente conciso, e assumidamente porque diz o autor na conversa com o narrador que o desfecho é de tal ordem e tão inesperado que merecia nada menos que 10 capítulos de exposição, mas que o autor se contenta com 1. O Machado de Assis aguentou a respiração.
Um português estranho, a caminhar na fronteira do estrangeiro, e também parece velho, tem ali uns traços, umas rugas que indicam isso.
Imaginem que tínhamos de escolher um livro para todos lermos. Para satisfazer igualmente a vontade de todos, podíamos fazer um livro grande e mau, o que acham? Ou um livro pequeno e bom, e cá está ele. É suficientemente leve para não se encalhar, mas suficientemente pesado para nos fazer parar para pensar.
Espero que com esta apresentação tenha conseguido passar a mesma ironia e tom humorístico que o livro me passou. É interessante que a história é contada como se o autor a tivesse escrito e estivesse a lê-la para nós; ele já sabe o que vai acontecer e conta-nos, tal como eu o faço.
Epah eu hoje acordei a pensar que era cómico, e o ser humano tem de pensar assim. Eu na minha cabeça sou o mais cómico, mesmo que não seja.
* The Origin of Species: The Difficulty of Evolution and Energy States :Philosophy:
A evolução não é fácil. Porque a tendência é gastar a menor energia e realizar o menor trabalho. Há a inércia filogenética/evolutiva que faz-nos querer ficar no lowest energy state. E a tendência é a taxa de evolução diminuir progressivamente (porque quanto mais energy tem o state em que se está, mais confortáveis nos sentimos em ficar lá). Somos "bons" e não sentimos tanta necessidade de melhorar, e ir de 99% da população para 99.9% é muito mais difícil que ir de 50 para 50.9%. Isto também ocorre porque maximizar a "produtividade" a partir de certo ponto é difícil.
Na evolução cada espécie ocupa o seu nicho, explora diferentes recursos de diferentes maneiras.
"Struggle for existence". Sexual selection results from a contest between rivals of the same sex.
Mudam-se os tempos, mudam-se as vontades - Camões. Os mais adaptados perpetuam a sua natureza, têm uma impressão digital mais definida, até que ninguém consegue adaptar-se ao ambiente, até que a melhor forma de estar no ambiente é de um modo discreto, como a impressão digital por detrás das luvas. (Contribuem, têm o seu nicho, são feitos de matéria orgânica) e a reprodução é manter a chama da vida, da impressão que impingimos no mundo. Essa poderá ser, em última análise, a chama que impele as ações da vida. "A mente é um caos de prazer" - Darwin.
* Alex in Numberland: Logarithmic Perception and Exponential Growth :Math:
We actually have a logarithmic perception of the world, our way of measuring stuff in our terms is logarithmic, as the indigenous show. And that explains that time gets faster as we age. We measure in ratios, and I would say psychology plays a big role in this, since our perception of more being less over repetition affects our behavior which affects our perception down the pipeline.
We sort of develop internal measuring mechanisms, standard measures. For example, ants that had their legs cut and put longer ones, would overshoot the distance; they measure by steps. We also measure with the circadian rhythm... And this ties back to the memory thing, to which I have an analogy: it's like a typewriter that writes, but as time goes on just stands by, drawing a line instead of writing. In sum, we need exponential growth/evolution to counter our natural tendency, and maybe that is why we have trouble understanding deeply exponential growth. Our ratios get played.
This way of counting ratios in approximation, in a way, is a consequence of the error being a matter of ratio, and to minimize the error one should maximize ratio intuition. You won't get exact answers, but pretty much every time you will give an accurate range.

* Alex in Numberland: Base 12, Cardinality, and Meta Numbers :Math:
Some people say a 12-number based system is actually more natural and better than the 10 we have. And this is legit insane, it's mind-boggling to think that it is a man-made construct that ten is the end of the digits. And it's because of fingers and hands, goddamn fingers and hands. Then I started thinking, wait, but ten multiplied by any number is just adding a zero, but with base twelve what happens? Cause that was sort of my justification for why we use base 10, it seems obvious, because I never thought of the possibility of 12 being the end of digits. And then I was like, wait, but does ten hold these properties in the 12 system? It should, it's the number, not the fact that it is the last digit, that makes it have that property.
But then what interesting mathematical facts are there in alternative number systems, like in arithmetic and stuff? Like there can be a study of alternative number systems, and the properties for the number systems of base belonging to R, or even negative numbers, who cares. It's meta numbers study.
And this is such a new thought, it's something that I had never thought about. And this makes the distinction between ordinality and cardinality make sense. Cause it's not the order that makes the properties, it's the cardinality, the quantity. You can have number 100 on the duodecimal system, but that is not the number who has the properties of 100. Since I had only thought about one system, for me ordinality and cardinality were blended. How have I never conceived this contingency? It's fucking wow.
And twelve can be better because of divisibility. It makes it easier because there are more integer portions. It makes multiplication tables more periodic, it even includes the pattern of 3, 6, 9, so maybe Tesla was referring indirectly to this thing of 12. And in reality hours are in 12: dozens, months, inches.

* Alex in Numberland: Language, Infinity, and the Normal Distribution :Math:
It's just so cool that there is so much to counting, and how language affects it. How we say numbers shows our past, our stuff.
Interesting that talking about origami, there was a guy who instead of making origami to make figures, just made it to find math theorems and stuff of that nature. It's like analytical geometry to geometry.
He talks about Indians, and their names for numbers. And the freaking Buddha knows numbers (in factors of 100) until 10 to the 421. It's of utter ridiculousness that he knows numbers until this, and the author points out that this obsession with the bigger and smaller numbers is metaphysical in nature.
Since pi seems to be the norm, in that it has a normal distribution, it is a sort of predetermined randomness, so it would be perfect for stuff that is better without patterns, such as deciding your poker move or stuff like that.

* The Animate and the Inanimate: Entropy, Symmetry, and the Reverse Universe :Physics:
That the probability of a universe in which entropy increases is equal to that of the opposite. It's basically a split decision, but once one of these is, then the Universe assembles. This assumed some form of, approximately, there not ever being a perfect transaction, always bits left whether that's energy lost or motion gained.
The interesting thing is that then William James Sidis conceptualizes a myriad of Universes like our own, some in reverse. It's then extremely likely that the overall tendency would be neutral, on average not building nor decaying.
Sofia points out a consequence of a direction, which I hadn't thought about directly, which is the visibility of, relative to an event, its cause and its effect. Since there is a redundancy in the case of a universe where entropy increases, we can look at an event both as a cause of a future event and as an effect of a past event. In our case of decaying, there will be more visibility on the past, maybe not more information (cause of conservation), but maybe more traceability, more definiteness. This ends up in an asymmetry in how easy it is to predict the future and predict the past from a given event. This leads to basically an arrow of pockets of reducibility. In the reverse universe, the reverse takes place.
He then says that in this reverse universe the explanation is teleological in nature. I would say that teleological is in a reverse order, but that the explanation for the reverse universe need not be theological; we eventually would figure out an explanation in causal terms, just like in our Universe, but the opposite, again, because laws (except for this one that divides the Universes) are reversible. He then goes right away acknowledging that it's just an appearance of theology. He then points out that the reverse Universe seems like it's full of life, only with a low probability would you get something that is dying.

* Simulacra and Simulation: The Hyperreal and the Murderous Science :Philosophy:
Baudrillard sets off by saying that the hyperreal, the simulation, has substituted the real. The map is more real than the territory. The representation becomes the reality. "The desert of the real itself."
He says that something got lost in the way, the charm of the map. I think he is saying the difference is so little it lost its flavor. Reducibility has taken its toll. The approximation of reality to a programmatic, objective and wrong model, kills the imaginary component, kills points of view. The hyperreal, a pure operational tool.
While dissimulation keeps the reality aspect intact, simulating makes distinction between true, false, real, and imaginary arbitrary. Says that images dissimulate the fact that there's nothing behind them, just like in SILO.
When simulation happens, everything is dead and resurrected, it has no profundity, it all snaps at the slightest movement, it's a thin sheet, fragile to the careful observer, a dead space only alive at its surface. Science, he says, is in this sense murderous.
Says that Disneyland is portrayed as imaginary to pretend that the other things are real. "This world wants to be childish in order to make us believe that the adults are elsewhere, in the 'real' world."

* Simulacra and Simulation: Historical Duplication, Hallucination, and the Matrix :Philosophy:
Baudrillard talks about this duplication, say in historical events, which were substituted by their representations as movies, and those camera angles, and those characters, and reality TV, a simulation, as if the cameras weren't there. Much of that idea that this pear that I now eat is the realest thing there is, that the pain Tony Ferguson says is the most real feeling, the rest you can hallucinate.
He also considers the abstractions of today's world as simulations which is true, again it's our collective belief that creates this thin sheet, hanging by a thread. Today the entire world hangs by a thread, money, corporations, all are built on top of each other, one small perturbation and the building collapses. We have been piling a deck of cards faster and faster, hoping it doesn't collapse. One could say the 2008 crisis was a mini collapse. In this sense animals live much more a real life than us, without imagination. And yet it seems that among animals we are the ones that escaped the matrix, that escaped our environment.
Now what Baudrillard hints to me is that this simulation in our world is much more haunting than what I here described, that's what I want to understand, what is the message. The issue may be losing the sense of truth, that may be the worst consequence. "TV renders true". Perhaps we are ever more hallucinated, with no conscience.

* Simulacra and Simulation: The Cancer of Collectivism and the Lost Real :Philosophy:
"Porno, more interesting in the metaphysical sense than in the sexual sense." Maybe he is referring to humans unconsciously building a bigger system than they can grasp, slaves to that system that they themselves created. Very Silo like. Play the game, don't let the game play you. Maybe what he is saying is that the system has gained a life of its own, and we feed him, we make it grow, we stuck ourselves in it. Maybe we are the greatest challenge to ourselves, cause instead of making us progress, we fool ourselves thinking that we are progressing, when it's our creation that has been feeding off of our ignorance. In this way we live in a simulation, the simulation has already been created, and we were its creators, and we are its victims.
Hard to not be part of the system; free soloing in this world doesn't seem to take you far. Kinda radical but yes, when we were living real life hunting in the caves perhaps all that there is today is arbitrary, it could've gone so many different ways. It's that dance between being in the system, so that one isn't totally forgotten, but escaping the system to do something new.
That cinema is recreating what itself created, is pretending to show the same history that it's destroying, cause now in the mind for the people the history becomes the movie. It's resurrecting a ghost. And remakes are resurrecting a ghost of itself. The reality of movies in the world being shadowed by their simulation in cinema.
Holocaust is one of the most simulated things. TV makes the holocaust have a life of its own, without dimension, and makes everyone think they know, when they don't know, they scratched the surface. Creates a distorted view of history, in the attempt of making it realistic. Hyperreal.
Is memory a simulation according to Baudrillard?

* Simulacra and Simulation: Hypercommodity, Information Overload, and the Clone :Philosophy:
Hypermarkets and hypercommodity. Objects of consumption are now tests, no longer a commodity, to see if you align with the code, they ask us the questions. A simulacra of a thin culture, temporary, is herein created. One loses himself in the social mask.
Ads look at you, "you look at yourself in it, mixed with the others, it is the activity of consumption, a game of splitting in two and doubling that CLOSES THE WORLD ON ITSELF".
Baudrillard says that we live in a world of more and more information, and less and less meaning, Kaczynski style. He thinks it's because information creates a hyperreality of communication; you think you are more connected with social media, but it's a proxy, a simulacra, it doesn't have substance. Even if you overload yourself with tons of information, there's no depth. Depth in your actions is important, not just genetic programmatic efficiency.
An implosion of message in the medium which itself implodes on the real. Medium thus destroys itself, there is a fusion of all these terms. Circularity of vectors, one thing points to itself, points at nothing essentially. Has lost direction by its very nature.
"A society purely transparent to itself." A genius metaphor that Baudrillard scratches the surface: says that cloning is none other than metastasis, a cancer of equal cells, ill at their core. Mass collectivism is this, the orientation, the matrix, is nothing but cancer. Cause the cells aren't perfect, at least yet, and probably never. They have mistakes, propagate and become the end of the organism.
Says that some people dream of holograms, clones of themselves, and that when it happens it will no longer be a dream, thus losing its charm. Fucking amazing: "when an object is exactly like another, it is no longer exactly like the other, it is a bit more exact."

* Simulacra and Simulation: Domesticated Animals and the Necessity of Pain :Philosophy:
That animals being industrially bred, without space, is very much the path that humans take in industrial society. Jesus, the similarities between this and the Unabomber are not coincidental. That there emerges a dimension for the mental equilibrium of the animals, their purpose is given back to them in some form. Interestingly, giving humans back this purity of life is either like Kaczynski going back, or perhaps creating not a simulacrum (which already permeates what there is) but an illusion, like the matrix in the Matrix movie. The ingenuity and innocence of humans not knowing that they are being industrially fed into the machines. An hallucination, an imaginary, a real to hide the real.
This relates to "pain is the realest feeling you can get". Like Joker says: see their moral, their code, it's a bad joke, dropped at the first sign of trouble. When the chips are down, these civilized people, they'll eat each other. When there's pain, all the imaginaries suddenly become irrelevant. When you are about to die, you are free from the simulacra. There's this inner cannibal inevitable part that flips the switch. That's why torture is done. All of a sudden one changes himself in the presence of pain. Rules don't matter anymore. Artificialities cease to exist, that's the most natural state a human can be in. Sport is a simulation of this, fighting is a simulation, sometimes, of this instinct. When people make time to win by decision, that's a simulacra, with reference to no reality whatsoever.
He defends that domesticating animals is even worse than killing them, hunting them and eating them, it's worthy of a greater disdain. Interesting, cause domesticating is in this sense taking out their fundamental liberty, sterilizing them of their meaning, only giving them technology in the form of ration, incubating them in a simulation of their real lives; they are not even worth our respect, they are there for entertainment, for charity. We humans are likewise domesticated by the system, not even worth punishment, just little experimentation rats, lab rats, strategic pawns in the advancement of technology. Quite radical, and one can obviously say that dogs are happy with their owners, but yea it's the opposing view being developed to its logical consequence.
Honestly, I'm going to say it right now, 6 February 2024, in the future, if humans go down the route that some of these authors describe, they will be disgusted at how bad we treated domesticated animals. This thing is a Zero, in Bryan Johnson's way of saying, it's so blatantly obvious and it's everywhere, and yet no-one sees it, or the collective hasn't. People haven't yet figured out that meaning transcends the usual longevity, technology-driven quality of life definition.
Baudrillard says that this madness is justified in our minds by the hypothesis of the unconscious. And the fact that animals don't speak is what dooms them.
Baudrillard follows his own philosophy. He is one of the realest writers I have found. No bullshit, just telling you his way, you may only understand 15 per cent, but you know it's not hyperreal. Very much like Bruce Lee. The most truthful books have the least amount of information, but huge upside in changing your life, or how you see things.

* The Perfect Crime: The Elusive Truth and The Robbery of the Real :Philosophy:
This refers to the way I'm reading Baudrillard; perhaps it is my merit that it's my way, maybe Baudrillard can do this to everyone. Man, this Baudrillard remains elusive. I am humbled when I confront his ideas, and so I should be. He presents me a kind of challenge, because of its nature, distinct from the other writers. I must dig to find the truth. His way of thinking obliges me to feel the strain of change. A Baudrillard reader must read his own thoughts if he stands a chance at producing some of his own. This distinction between your thoughts and the thoughts you come up with is often overlooked. You have all the merit or disgrace. The reading differentiates itself because it isn't reactionary, as virtually all are, but actionary. It also demands the ever-fading silence from your mind. A sharp quiet proves necessary for your intentions. It is the exact opposite of a Spinoza.
This "perfect crime" suggests to me a consequence of simulacra and simulation. The robbery of the real, perfect crime for its substitution is perfect, more real than the real, the hyperreal, thus going unnoticed. Esvaziando a realidade, colapsando-a nas suas dimensões.

* The Perfect Crime: Preface, Speed, and the Loss of the Unknown :Philosophy:
That speed brings locations closer, predictability brings times closer, the will for realism brings reality and hyperreality closer. Times are becoming further apart, through the exponential evolution, or sigmoid. The distance between places is bound to keep on decreasing, until there is hardly a place in the whole of this planet that takes the work once done to reach the Everest for the first time. Unknown journeys will decay, until all is simply connected. This happens until we venture out of this planet, or out of this body of land of ours. Deep space and deep sea still will provide terrain for discovery for some time.
Diversity in our species is evolutionary. Previously there were mountains that separated peoples for centuries, and like a company with multiple teams, those people, with no way of communicating, would find their own way of solving the problems of the valley. We are bringing us closer thus bringing us alike. There was no Google or medium from which to see how others solved a given problem. I wonder if AI isolation will make some never leave their bubble and others make their own bubble. Those that like to follow will follow the AI and lose themselves to its will. The others will get up to see themselves as all they have.
Hyperreality gives continuity to the world, makes us a single beat.
"Só se pode legar aquilo que não se rentabiliza inteiramente". If you aimed for the perfect finish there is no legacy left for the world. That privilege lies only in those who finished before they were finished. Only needs living after death what had yet to live. Perhaps being and living the complete life is about leaving nothing for after. Exhausting your life. As we live longer and longer we increase the standard for what this means. God takes the best of you through your unwavering unwillingness to do the end yourself. What a disrespect towards your life to not end it yourself. To keep on raising the bet, more and more, until it bursts, and you lose everything.

* The Perfect Crime: Murder of Reality and Extermination of Illusion :Philosophy:
O assassínio da realidade e o extermínio da ilusão: Man this guy Baudrillard really escaped the matrix he helped shine light onto. From the inside the cage isn't visible.
The perfect crime in that everything is taken away yet without leaving any trace. It would be the perfect crime if, for example, we now were living in a full blown simulation made by us. That the last semblance of the real world was seen somewhere in 2017 or whatever, that reality extinguished around that area. Like the dead internet theory. Or creating a world for ourselves but then erasing our memories of the original world. What makes these crimes so perfect is that they are so close to being true yet so far. The closest to them is there not being a crime at all. It's a discontinuity. An infinitesimal to the other side and you enter the territory that there is no crime, you see the theory of the crime as conspiracy and hallucination. 
Says that the crime is never perfect cause the world betrays itself by having evidences of its own inexistence. Even in human endeavors, we feel the need to say something, thereby making the crime imperfect, thereby existing. How can we see that the perfect exists if its existence means that it can't be seen? We can only aspire to see the imperfect existing. The unawareness of the committed crime makes it perfect. One ought to look at evidences for existence of this world as much as evidences for the opposite. Simulated characters in games throw various evidences that their world exists, but what are the evidences that it doesn't? It is hard to escape inside out. It requires some perspective shift to really believe that some occurrence proves your world is not real. Mario seeing Snake being played in his world. How can he guess the concept of a video game to understand that he is in one too? Inside by Playdead. There is an exploit in his world that allows him to be free from the user that controls him.
That a clue of the illusion of reality is how submissive it is to us. We mold it, we make hypotheses to which it obeys. Evidências que a realidade apenas está mas não é. That reality has no personality, no character. To rescue reality, to see the leftovers of imperfection of the crime, we have to subtract everything to itself, navigate in the place and not in the map. Not just that the world disappeared, but that the question can't be put, says Baudrillard, that is the death of reality. When no fluctuation no matter how improbable can change the order of things, reality has long died. That things cannot aspire to present themselves in any other way except illusions of themselves. 
Now Baudrillard in this section seems to me to point out that the world died even before it started, in that he is saying that the real backbone world has disappeared in the world we created by being humans, by having eyes. Sort of the artificial death after the natural death of the world. Both are near perfect crimes.
* The Origin of Species: Artificial Selection and Human Nature :Philosophy:
Nós humanos temos a habilidade de definir a nossa natureza, pelo que temos a habilidade de adaptação à vontade. Temos por isso uma seleção artificial em dois sentidos. Podemos alterar a nossa "força" num ambiente. E escolhemos pessoas por aspeto, cor...
Se/quando houver manipulação genética, é uma seleção artificial de um modo artificial. É mais um nível de círculos concêntricos de seleções artificiais. Teremos na sociedade um emaranhado de seleções artificiais.
* Ecce Homo: Autopilot and the Purity of the Zone :Philosophy:
Nietzschean: When in autopilot, in the zone, enjoy the living hell out of it. That's in some sense the fakest thing and yet the closest to reality you get. So yes, a priori reasoning is finally complete, and it is complete. He who he is (Ecce homo), Nietzsche's purity.

* Ecce Homo: Walking, Caeiro, and the Unity of Being :Philosophy:
When Caeiro says he knows the truth, it's in the duality of reality that he finds himself. A reality where your perception is enhanced, your soul open, like when you grasp the world and every one of the cells of your fingertips, you feel yourself in and as the world, as one plain sentence, a melody of reality and an harmony of being, where perception stops being and just is. You forget time, the hardest of necessities.
Nietzsche suggests that one will never think truly and thoroughly when he is sitting. In walking there is a unification of body, mind, and the world.

* Ecce Homo: The World as a Reflexive Matrix :Philosophy:
People are afraid of the world, it's abusive from within. It's that individualistic Nietzsche style brute honesty reflective to oneself. Sometimes people go to the world to find themselves; the matrix that they created may have hints about what it is that people should search for in themselves. Like dreams, that some claim give you access to your mind, what happens in the world may be a hint for your journey, may inspire the reason. It's a reflexive projective world, that in which you live in, because you live in it. To pretend they moved their asses.
I am glad I was able to experience this moment, to be given the opportunity of being a human.

* Ecce Homo: Sleep, Cycles, and the Dual Space of Possibility :Life:
Take advantage of the natural way we live our days (sleep and live). See it as a game of mutual change and cycle, building a connected reality. A dual space of possibility. Yet have the bravery to not see discontinuity between these.
It's like this interpretation makes an eigenbasis. Make yourself to tomorrow as tomorrow was yesterday, that's what sleep gives you: the opportunity, yet not without cost. Measure the world and you yourself have changed and changed it.

* Ecce Homo: The Intentional Mind and Bruce Lee's Extraction :Life:
A focused mind shall be intentional in building itself. Hence they say: forget the world to see it, forget yourself to see yourself. Be enough wary of what you allow in your world and mind, for it might enslave you, and yet transparent, so as to be free in it. Be uncontrollably free but controllably yourself. Forever in control.
He who doesn't go "à deriva" will be going the route of something not his own, for in not deciding one is still. And still undisputed. Truly knowing what you want and where you're headed and why requires the strongest of knowledge about oneself, requires truth in life. Apply Bruce Lee's thought to the world: get what you can from it, get what you have to get from it, no more, and be quick outta there.

* Ecce Homo: Books as Mirrors and the Simulated World :Philosophy:
If there ever was a thing that had drawn itself it would be your mind and the world and what they make of each other. Hence they are but one, as in Buddha.
To read Nietzsche how he wants, you have to hate him. You cannot read more in a book than that which you already know.
Forever in control, you become God almighty, you create yourself and equivalently (implication flowing both ways), the world.
Math is discovered in the world but invented in your mind. The world is then not real, just a simulation a la Baudrillard to dissimulate that it is the mind that's real. Not just books, the world itself: because you see it through your mind, you cannot find there more than what you have within. The world is just there so you build yourself. You can run from it, hide from it, but destiny will arrive the same, and your destiny is here, or should I say, it's you.
People have this weird way of being domesticated pets of which they are their own owners.

* Ecce Homo: Zeroth Principles and the Inner Visionary :Philosophy:
Therefore zeros are not to be found in books, even tautologically. You can start there, but ultimately, you must face the mirror. Zeroth principles came from within, they don't leave a trace. Perhaps if you use the system it will know it was you. In that sort of fashion, to escape it, don't let it know, be a visionary from inside out; when it talks to you it doesn't know it is dealing with a Kant. There is a certain mystique to Shakespeare's "have more than you show, say less than you know". Might explain why one gets the feeling that there lacks authenticity and originality today.

* Ecce Homo: Amor Fati and the Blank Paper of Reality :Philosophy:
Amor fati, remorse, blatant anticipation, Nietzsche says, is pointless. Run with what is, for that's what you got.
The book is just there so you can read and see your mind. The world isn't there when you are by yourself—not just when you are alone, but when you are by yourself. It was made from you for you to you. Read for your own benefit; history there is already enough, historians there are enough.
Right here right now, my page was full, I pondered if that means the reason I had a "creative block" for some minutes. I took off a blank one and voilà, reality realized itself, same as I reshaped it, same as my mind reshaped itself. For him to write give him a blank paper. For him to speak feed him silence, for him to think feed him boredom. Give yourself a blank paper and you shall write. Give yourself the opportunity to make the world come to existence, make the world happen.

* Ecce Homo: Humans as Unaware Gods and Frictionless Surfaces :Life:
A human is nothing short of a God who doesn't know what it is.
Simulations are abundant, scarce is a level zero surface. Sleepy when reading chemistry, Nietzsche waked me up, and for that I thank him, but mostly to myself. Then I read that one phrase, OF THE LIKES: IN BOOKS ONE FINDS ONLY WHAT HE ALREADY KNOWS, and boom spaghetti.
Don't neglect these signs about the world and yourself. When sleepy it means enough of the world, like bro stop trying to walk on a frictionless surface. Even if you may not be ready for the day, it cannot always be night.

* Ecce Homo: Meta-Reflection on Capturing Thoughts :Writing:
Jesus fucking christ these erroneous thoughts make me feel like a fucking misunderstood genius that not even myself can understand 5 weeks later, when going from paper to computer. Dude some of the stuff you have to write more clearly bro, like some of the phrases may be lost forever.
This was all done in a trip bus from Lisbon to Portimão, fucking awesome in 2 pieces of paper, 22/2/2024.
Came from "Ultimately no one can hear in things - books included - more than he already knows."
How I would love to hear the real words of Nietzsche. Forever he will be a glimpse in my eyes.
* The Origin of Species: Population Dynamics and r/K Strategists :Life:
Na dinâmica de populações atinge-se um equilíbrio. Quando as espécies atingem a sua capacidade de carregamento (K), se aumentarem, vai haver competição por alimento, etc., e morrem alguns. Depois fica abaixo da capacidade de carregamento. Por isso aumenta outra vez. E este é mais um ciclo. Não podes controlar que irão haver ups and downs, só podes controlar quão altos serão os ups e quão baixos serão os downs.
És tu um estratega r ou um estratega K? Queres fazer muitas coisas no mundo (r-strategist) mas saber que só algumas sobreviverão, ou queres fazer algumas mas cuidar bem delas (K-strategist)? Quem vive mais no r vive com mais imprevisibilidade. Queres andar constantemente a oscilar o tamanho da população (bactérias) ou oscilar pouco mas quando desce poder haver esticão (baleias)? Imprevisibilidade, visto que não temos uma boa noção dos fatores que farão com que a seleção natural selecione as coisas que fizemos. Nos K, ou são aqueles ou nada.
The sigmoid curve: "most populations exhibit a sigmoid growth curve, which implies a relatively slow start in growth, a rapid increase, and then a leveling off when the carrying capacity of the species' environment is reached."
* Ecce Homo: Autopilot and the Purity of the Zone :Philosophy:
Nietzschean: When in autopilot, in the zone, enjoy the living hell out of it. That's in some sense the fakest thing and yet the closest to reality you get. So yes, a priori reasoning is finally complete, and it is complete. He who he is (Ecce homo), Nietzsche's purity.

* Ecce Homo: Walking, Caeiro, and the Unity of Being :Philosophy:
When Caeiro says he knows the truth, it's in the duality of reality that he finds himself. A reality where your perception is enhanced, your soul open, like when you grasp the world and every one of the cells of your fingertips, you feel yourself in and as the world, as one plain sentence, a melody of reality and an harmony of being, where perception stops being and just is. You forget time, the hardest of necessities.
Nietzsche suggests that one will never think truly and thoroughly when he is sitting. In walking there is a unification of body, mind, and the world.

* Ecce Homo: The World as a Reflexive Matrix :Philosophy:
People are afraid of the world, it's abusive from within. It's that individualistic Nietzsche style brute honesty reflective to oneself. Sometimes people go to the world to find themselves; the matrix that they created may have hints about what it is that people should search for in themselves. Like dreams, that some claim give you access to your mind, what happens in the world may be a hint for your journey, may inspire the reason. It's a reflexive projective world, that in which you live in, because you live in it. To pretend they moved their asses.
I am glad I was able to experience this moment, to be given the opportunity of being a human.

* Ecce Homo: Sleep, Cycles, and the Dual Space of Possibility :Life:
Take advantage of the natural way we live our days (sleep and live). See it as a game of mutual change and cycle, building a connected reality. A dual space of possibility. Yet have the bravery to not see discontinuity between these.
It's like this interpretation makes an eigenbasis. Make yourself to tomorrow as tomorrow was yesterday, that's what sleep gives you: the opportunity, yet not without cost. Measure the world and you yourself have changed and changed it.

* Ecce Homo: The Intentional Mind and Bruce Lee's Extraction :Life:
A focused mind shall be intentional in building itself. Hence they say: forget the world to see it, forget yourself to see yourself. Be enough wary of what you allow in your world and mind, for it might enslave you, and yet transparent, so as to be free in it. Be uncontrollably free but controllably yourself. Forever in control.
He who doesn't go "à deriva" will be going the route of something not his own, for in not deciding one is still. And still undisputed. Truly knowing what you want and where you're headed and why requires the strongest of knowledge about oneself, requires truth in life. Apply Bruce Lee's thought to the world: get what you can from it, get what you have to get from it, no more, and be quick outta there.

* Ecce Homo: Books as Mirrors and the Simulated World :Philosophy:
If there ever was a thing that had drawn itself it would be your mind and the world and what they make of each other. Hence they are but one, as in Buddha.
To read Nietzsche how he wants, you have to hate him. You cannot read more in a book than that which you already know.
Forever in control, you become God almighty, you create yourself and equivalently (implication flowing both ways), the world.
Math is discovered in the world but invented in your mind. The world is then not real, just a simulation a la Baudrillard to dissimulate that it is the mind that's real. Not just books, the world itself: because you see it through your mind, you cannot find there more than what you have within. The world is just there so you build yourself. You can run from it, hide from it, but destiny will arrive the same, and your destiny is here, or should I say, it's you.
People have this weird way of being domesticated pets of which they are their own owners.

* Ecce Homo: Zeroth Principles and the Inner Visionary :Philosophy:
Therefore zeros are not to be found in books, even tautologically. You can start there, but ultimately, you must face the mirror. Zeroth principles came from within, they don't leave a trace. Perhaps if you use the system it will know it was you. In that sort of fashion, to escape it, don't let it know, be a visionary from inside out; when it talks to you it doesn't know it is dealing with a Kant. There is a certain mystique to Shakespeare's "have more than you show, say less than you know". Might explain why one gets the feeling that there lacks authenticity and originality today.

* Ecce Homo: Amor Fati and the Blank Paper of Reality :Philosophy:
Amor fati, remorse, blatant anticipation, Nietzsche says, is pointless. Run with what is, for that's what you got.
The book is just there so you can read and see your mind. The world isn't there when you are by yourself—not just when you are alone, but when you are by yourself. It was made from you for you to you. Read for your own benefit; history there is already enough, historians there are enough.
Right here right now, my page was full, I pondered if that means the reason I had a "creative block" for some minutes. I took off a blank one and voilà, reality realized itself, same as I reshaped it, same as my mind reshaped itself. For him to write give him a blank paper. For him to speak feed him silence, for him to think feed him boredom. Give yourself a blank paper and you shall write. Give yourself the opportunity to make the world come to existence, make the world happen.

* Ecce Homo: Humans as Unaware Gods and Frictionless Surfaces :Life:
A human is nothing short of a God who doesn't know what it is.
Simulations are abundant, scarce is a level zero surface. Sleepy when reading chemistry, Nietzsche waked me up, and for that I thank him, but mostly to myself. Then I read that one phrase, OF THE LIKES: IN BOOKS ONE FINDS ONLY WHAT HE ALREADY KNOWS, and boom spaghetti.
Don't neglect these signs about the world and yourself. When sleepy it means enough of the world, like bro stop trying to walk on a frictionless surface. Even if you may not be ready for the day, it cannot always be night.

* Ecce Homo: Meta-Reflection on Capturing Thoughts :Writing:
Jesus fucking christ these erroneous thoughts make me feel like a fucking misunderstood genius that not even myself can understand 5 weeks later, when going from paper to computer. Dude some of the stuff you have to write more clearly bro, like some of the phrases may be lost forever.
This was all done in a trip bus from Lisbon to Portimão, fucking awesome in 2 pieces of paper, 22/2/2024.
Came from "Ultimately no one can hear in things - books included - more than he already knows."
How I would love to hear the real words of Nietzsche. Forever he will be a glimpse in my eyes.
* The Origin of Species: Finding Inspiration Right Before Your Eyes :Life:
Applications. Search for applications of everything. Darwin applied all the philosophers (principles of geology for example) and applied them to his own. Because sometimes the base of the greatest idea of your life is right before YOUR FUCKING EYES!!! He read a book about the behavior of populations for "amusing" and from there he discovered his thesis. Just for fun he found his greatest idea.
* Human, All Too Human: Earning Your Thoughts and Fasting from Books :Writing:
"I was released from the book, and read nothing more for years - the greatest favour I ever done myself! - that nethermost self, as if buried alive, as if made mute beneath the constant need to pay heed to other selves (which is what reading is)."
It would be smart to at some point of my life do this. No consumption, the amount of free time for creation would be immense. Thinking purely off of yourself. Earning your thoughts. Earn your words. Hoje a palavra sai barato. Já há dez anos saía, mas hoje em dia o mercado está muito em baixo. LLMs tornam a palavra corrente numa fonte de palavras. Há abastecimento para meio mundo e mais metade dele. 
"We do not belong to those who have their ideas from books, stimulated by books." His ideas come from walks on mountains and trails that themselves become thoughtful.

* Human, All Too Human: Repeating History and Reading the Future :Philosophy:
His explanation of inspiration comes very close to converging to mine. Or I perceived this idea and it came to fruition after reading the passage. Nietzsche, I'm here, don't feel so lonely now. And in writing this I realize we glorify those of the past, as in we breed from the past. Maybe no wonder we make the same mistakes as them, no wonder that we learn from history that we don't learn from history, that those who don't know history are doomed to repeat it. But let's not forget ourselves. Those who only know history are also doomed to repeat it. History may be there to shape, but not to make history now. If I can somehow read those of the future, know the future so that I'm not condemned to build it the way it is...

* Human, All Too Human: Eternal Recurrence and the Will to Live :Philosophy:
In being immortal one will die several times, he proposes. So is eternal recurrence immortality? Is it to ask that a life is meaningful for you to ask if being immortal with it wouldn't cause you a death of will to live? What if we allow ourselves to the idea of eternal recurrence as the only way to stay living? That this life you choose to live, and in the others you are, a la Sisyphus, condemned to relive it. But not passively. You would have to intend living, for not living it the way it was would be the reason of your death. 
They say death gives meaning to life, but what if it is the ability of a life to escape death, in that it doesn't need death to be meaningful, that makes it meaningful. And could one succumb to eternal recurrence? I think it would be very very hard, for you are playing against infinity. Victory would never happen, defeat at any time. It seems reasonable to say there would be people at life #3000, life #10 googols, life at any number, yet no one at infinity.
Interesting would it be to see how many people would just live life once, in that they would rather live free for the whole of their existence. Or live under the illusion of freedom, memory being erased. How many would sign up for eternal recurrence if they had no choice but to relive their life forever? I mean in a sense that's slavery. Is there any meaningful difference between this eternal recurrence and our lives right now? Are they not topologically equivalent? Maybe time is the biggest difference. Victory is prolonged, unwarning, defeat is immediate and defining. And the sheer curiosity building up as to what lies beyond the recurrence would be enough for people to give it up.
If will to live is finite, but unlimited, people die with so much left, so much unexplored territory. The perfect finish would have to die as will to live is redlining. But this is very relative.

* Human, All Too Human: The Overhuman, Creation, and Smelling the Lie :Philosophy:
"I walk among human beings as among fragments of the future. The future that I envisage."
"Thus I willed it." This, were it not written by this man already, would have been a good lemma for my conquer. As in veni vidi vici.
"What would there be to create if gods existed?" Power on us, we will the world, we can create. And if we don't create, others will will for us our creation into existence, and our existence will de-will itself out of it. Did we will ourselves into existence?
My words are lightning bolts, and you've been thunderstruck. When you are so good that you are now only competing with those that don't exist, with the myths separated by the barrier of time.
"My genius is in my nostrils." "Smell lie as lie." "Why am I a destiny." That in a sense is a question you must seek, a self-evident axiom of your life were you to believe it.
The yes-saying with the no-doing I refuse. That in trying to push the herd of sheep to comply to morality we are sacrificing truth, sacrificing change.

* Human, All Too Human: The Overhuman, Evil, and Seeing Reality :Philosophy:
What he is concerned is that the highest humans would consider his overman devil. So the highest humans, he thinks, should have a dark side. Perhaps they first rose because of the good, then despite it. Those that show dark side early on don't get fed enough, they get fed enough to get them hanging on life by a thread, the system of good wants to hold them down.
